region_of_origin: East Asia (derived from red algae; widely used across Asia)

main_ingredients: red algae (Gelidium spp. or Gracilaria spp.)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dried agar powder is approximately 3 g (one teaspoon), used as a gelling agent in desserts, jellies, and beverages.

nutrient_source.title: Agar, dried — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry for dried agar powder (F14050001). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for dried agar powder exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Agar is composed predominantly of agarose and agaropectin polysaccharides that are largely indigestible and function as dietary fibre. The GI of agar gel has been estimated at approximately 11 in peer-reviewed literature (analogous to other non-digestible polysaccharide gelling agents). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (11 × 80.88 × 0.03) / 100 ≈ 0.27, rounded to 0.3 for a 3 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high potassium (1125 mg/100g) and calcium (625 mg/100g) with moderate sodium (102 mg/100g); outstanding DASH-aligned mineral profile
hypertension.guidance: Dried agar powder provides an exceptional potassium-to-sodium ratio supporting blood pressure regulation; use unsweetened in preparations to avoid negating the cardiovascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High dietary fibre (7.7 g/100g) slows glucose absorption; per-serving carbohydrate load is negligible at typical 2–5 g usage
type2_diabetes.guidance: Unsweetened agar-based preparations are suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to very low glycemic load per serving (GL ≈ 0.3 for 3 g); avoid sweetened agar desserts as added sugar substantially raises glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.0 g/100g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre, and exceptional potassium and calcium supporting vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dried agar powder is cardioprotective at typical serving sizes due to its zero cholesterol, negligible fat, high soluble fibre content, and outstanding DASH mineral profile; it is suitable as a gelling agent in heart-healthy diets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome immune modulation; exceptional calcium (625 mg/100g) for bone protection; negligible saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried agar powder is suitable for people with rheumatoid arthritis as a supportive ingredient; ensure preparations are unsweetened to preserve anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried agar powder is a plant-based gelling agent derived from red algae (primarily Gelidium and Gracilaria species), processed by drying and milling into a fine white powder. It is widely used across East and Southeast Asian cuisines as a vegetarian and vegan substitute for gelatin in desserts, jellies, puddings, and beverages such as grass jelly drinks and almond tofu. Unlike gelatin, agar sets firmly at room temperature and produces a firmer, more brittle texture. Nutritionally, dried agar powder is energy-dense at 306 kcal/100g due to its high carbohydrate content (80.88 g/100g), but typical culinary servings are only 2–5 g, making the per-serving caloric and glycemic impact negligible. It provides 7.7 g dietary fibre per 100g, supporting gut health and glucose regulation. Its mineral profile is exceptional: potassium at 1125 mg/100g and calcium at 625 mg/100g make it highly DASH-aligned for hypertension management. Iron content is notably high at 21.4 mg/100g. Cholesterol is zero and saturated fat is negligible, making it suitable for cardiovascular and diabetic dietary patterns. Agar is gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher. It is used in Singapore, China, Japan, Malaysia, and across Southeast Asia as a staple gelling ingredient in both home cooking and commercial food production.
common_user_queries: What is agar powder made from?
Is agar powder the same as gelatin?
How much agar powder per cup of liquid?
Is agar powder good for high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of agar?
Can I use agar powder for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14050001) at confidence 0.95. The food is a well-characterised processed ingredient with a canonical identity. GI is estimated from peer-reviewed analogous data for non-digestible polysaccharide gels, clearly documented.
notes: saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo source; agar contains negligible saturated fatty acids as a polysaccharide-dominant seaweed extract. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 g/100g: naturally zero as this is a plant-derived polysaccharide with no animal-derived components. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for dried agar powder (F14050001) as of 2026-04-16. GI is an analogous estimate (≈11) based on peer-reviewed literature for non-digestible polysaccharide gelling agents; no direct GI measurement for dried agar powder exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dietary_tag 'wholegrain' from the input has been removed as agar is a seaweed-derived polysaccharide and does not qualify as a wholegrain food.
